title: Asian Elephants and Comparison with African Elephants
focus: Characteristics, habitat, conservation status of Asian Elephants, and morphological differences from African Elephants.
rationale: This section covers all the provided information on elephants, forming a distinct biological and conservation topic separate from crocodiles.
sectionExcerpt: Asian Elephants (Elephas maximus) are the largest land mammals in Asia, featuring a matriarchal social structure and a 22-month gestation period. They are classified as Endangered by IUCN, Schedule I under WPA, and Appendix I under CITES. Project Elephant was launched in 1992. Key differences from African elephants include smaller size, twin-domed head, smaller rounded ears, humped back, and tusks typically only visible in some males.
evidenceBullets: Asian Elephants have a matriarchal social structure and the longest gestation period in mammals at 22 months.
Conservation Status of Asian Elephants: IUCN Endangered, WPA 1972 Schedule I, and CITES Appendix I.
Project Elephant was launched in 1992, and there are currently 33 Elephant Reserves across 14 major states.
Asian elephants have a twin-domed head with a central indent and a humped/convex back, whereas African elephants have a rounded/flat single dome and a concave/dipped back.
Only some male Asian elephants have visible tusks, while both male and female African elephants have larger tusks.

title: Crocodile Conservation Project and Native Species of India
focus: 50 years of the Crocodile Conservation Project, recovery statistics, and the characteristics, habitats, and conservation statuses of Saltwater, Mugger, and Gharial crocodiles.
rationale: Groups the 50th anniversary of the conservation project with the detailed breakdown of the three native crocodilian species, as their habitats, statuses, and recovery efforts are deeply interconnected.
sectionExcerpt: India celebrated 50 years of its Crocodile Conservation Project, launched in 1975. Odisha is the only state with conservation centres for all three native species: Saltwater (Dangamal), Mugger (Ramatirtha), and Gharial (Tikarpada). India holds 80% of the global wild gharial population. The three native species are the Saltwater Crocodile (Least Concern), Mugger (Vulnerable), and Gharial (Critically Endangered). Gharials are expert fish catchers with a unique 'Ghara' on males' snouts, while Muggers use tools and dig burrows.
evidenceBullets: The Crocodile Conservation Project was launched in April 1975 based on H.R. Bustard's recommendations.
Odisha is the only Indian state with conservation centres for all 3 native species: Tikarpada for gharials, Dangamal for saltwater crocodiles, and Ramatirtha for muggers.
Saltwater Crocodile (Estuarine): IUCN Least Concern; found in brackish water and mangroves like Bhitarkanika, Andaman & Nicobar, and Sundarbans.
Mugger (Marsh Crocodile): IUCN Vulnerable; known to use tools like balance sticks to lure birds and is the only Indian species that digs burrows on land.
Gharial: IUCN Critically Endangered; adult males have a bulb-like 'Ghara' on their snout; found in deep, fast-flowing rivers like Chambal, Girwa, and Son.
India holds 80% of the global wild gharial population, supported by sanctuaries like the National Chambal Sanctuary.
